text: This film shows a minimally invasive surgical approach for a thymectomy in patients with myasthenia gravis.
The surgeon utilizes a robot-assisted videothoracoscopy to perform a thymectomy, removing the thymus and anterior mediastinal fat through a left transpleuric approach.
The chest X-ray is important to verify the feasibility of the transpleuric approach. The X-ray does not show any evident pleuro parenchymal inflammation.
The CT scan shows a hyperplasia of the thymus and excludes any tumoral pathology.
The patient is positioned left side up at a thirty-degree angle with a shoulder roll and a bean bag, and ventilated using a double lumen endotracheal tube.
Women patients should have the mammary gland dislocated towards the center to ensure improved surgical access and better cosmetic results.

The surgeon identifies the intercostal spaces, beginning at the eleventh space and progressing to the fifth space, where two of the three thoracoscopic ports are positioned for the robot-assisted videothoracoscopy.
The surgeon performs an initial skin dissection at the anterior axillary line, creating an incision approximately two centimeters in length.
The surgeon manually tests the axis of the pleural space, assessing its orientation and condition within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon interrupts lung ventilation and introduces a trocar connected to the CO₂ insufflation system into the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon inserts the camera through the thoracoscopic port to perform an initial inspection of the pleural space, observing the anatomical structures and surgical field.
The endothoracic view reveals the locations of the two additional thoracoscopic port sites within the pleural space.
Two port sites are visible: one located in the fifth intercostal space along the midclavicular line, and another in the third intercostal space along the midaxillary line.
The robotic surgical system is positioned at a forty-five-degree angle relative to the patient's axis, ready for the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon explores the mediastinic surface of the left pleural space using the robotic operative arms, identifying anatomical structures within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies the pericardium, phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels within the thoracic cavity using the robotic camera.
The surgeon begins the operation by removing the fat tissue located at the cardiophrenic angle, using robotic instruments visible through the endothoracic view.
The surgeon makes an incision from the bottom to the top of the retrosternal part of the mediastinal pleura, followed by the dissection of thymic tissue from the retrosternal space.
The surgeon isolates the thymus from the pericardic surface, progressing from the bottom to the top, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon makes a pleural incision at the apical level to access the area containing the mammary vessels and the phrenic nerve.
Great care must be taken to isolate the tissue of this region in order to avoid injury of the nerve.
The surgeon mobilizes the thymus gland from the bottom to the top, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues within the mediastinal space.
The surgeon identifies the cervical poles of the thymus in the neck region, visually distinguishing these anatomical structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully locates the innominate veins, ensuring precise identification within the surgical field during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the cervical poles with careful traction after successfully isolating the inominate veins, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a maneuver on the left cervical pole, manipulating the tissue in the neck region with visible precision.
The surgeon applies downward traction to visualize the thymic vein branches, which are then doubly clipped and transected.
The surgeon completes the dissection of thymic tissue from the right mediastinal pleura, separating the thymus from the surrounding pleural tissue.
Great care must be taken in this stage so as to avoid injury to the right phrenic nerve.
The surgeon introduces an endobag into the pleural space through the inferior port, preparing to collect resected thymic tissue.
The surgeon places the excised thymus into the endobag within the pleural space and removes it through the inferior port.
The surgeon places a chest tube into the pleural space, followed by reinflation of the lung, which visibly expands within the thoracic cavity.
The two maneuvers can be verified with the aid of the optical view.
